d5s0.what: You're driving into the caldera of a supervolcano. Yellowstone last erupted 640,000 years ago with a force 1,000× Mount St. Helens; the 70-km-wide crater it left IS the park's central plateau, and the magma chamber below still powers every geyser you'll see. The East Entrance road over Sylvan Pass (2,600 m) was Teddy Roosevelt's favorite approach — 'the most scenic 50 miles in America.'
see: Sylvan Pass — avalanche country, snow walls linger into July some years
First glimpse of Yellowstone Lake from the descent — pull over, everyone does
tips: Show the parks pass + photo ID at the booth
Fill the tank in Cody before this — in-park gas exists but is pricey and sparse
From the gate to West Yellowstone is ~130 mi but budget the WHOLE day — 'bison jam' is not a metaphor
Cell signal dies at the gate; you downloaded offline maps, right?
d5s0.fact: The ground in the caldera breathes: GPS stations show it rising and falling several cm a year as magma shifts 8 km below your tires. Scientists watch it constantly; it is not due — 'overdue supervolcano' is tabloid myth.

d5s1.what: North America's largest high-altitude lake: 350 km² of water at 2,357 m, average temperature 5°C even in July — survival time if you swim is under 30 minutes, which is why nobody does. Fishing Bridge was once THE place to catch cutthroat trout, shoulder-to-shoulder; fishing was banned in 1973 to save the spawning run, and the name stayed.
see: Stand on Fishing Bridge and look down — native cutthroat trout still school over the gravel in July
Lake Hotel (1891), 10 min west: the oldest hotel in the park, string quartet in the lobby some evenings — walk in, it's free to gawk
tips: The general store at Fishing Bridge is a good cold-drinks-and-restrooms stop
Steam rising from the water along the shore = underwater geothermal vents; the lake floor has its own geysers
d5s1.fact: Sonar mapping found a 30-m bulge on the lake floor that scientists monitor — the lake bottom is as geothermally alive as the geyser basins, just hidden.

d5s2.what: Hayden Valley is an old lakebed — the lake was once much bigger — and its clay soil won't grow trees, which is why it's a treeless savanna where you can see for kilometres. That visibility is why it's the best wildlife theatre in the lower 48: bison herds, elk, grizzlies on the far slopes, and the Wapiti Lake wolf pack.
see: Bison herds doing exactly as they please, including using the road
Dawn/dusk: scan the far treelines with binoculars for wolves — reintroduced in 1995 after a 70-year absence, one of conservation's greatest comebacks
Grizzly Overlook (unofficial name, the big pullout mid-valley) — where the spotting-scope crowd gathers each evening; they'll usually let you look
tips: 25 m from bison and elk, 100 m from bears and wolves — these are legal minimums, rangers ticket for less
If a bison jam stops you, stay in the truck, shift to park, enjoy it — average delay 10–20 min
Late afternoon light + animals = your best wildlife photos of the trip; shoot from the window
d5s2.fact: When wolves returned in 1995, the elk stopped loitering in river valleys, willows and aspens regrew, beavers returned, and even the rivers changed shape. Ecologists call it a trophic cascade; you're driving through the textbook example.

d5s3.what: The Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone: 300 m deep, carved through rhyolite that hot acidic groundwater cooked soft and yellow — those mustard, rose and white walls are literally the 'yellow stone' the park is named for. The Lower Falls drop 94 m, twice Niagara's height.
see: Artist Point itself — the postcard view up-canyon to the falls; Thomas Moran's 1872 painting from near here convinced Congress to invent the idea of a national park
Green stripe at the falls' lip — a notch of harder rock that stays deeper water
If knees allow: Uncle Tom's area viewpoints across the river on the way out
tips: Late-afternoon light hits the canyon walls best — you're arriving at the right time
The viewpoint is 100 m from the parking lot — this one is effort-free
Osprey nest on the canyon spires most summers; scan the pinnacles
d5s3.fact: Moran's massive canvas of this canyon was bought by Congress for $10,000 and hung in the Capitol — the first landscape painting ever purchased by the US government, and the reason Yellowstone became the world's first national park six months later.

d6s0.what: Not the biggest geyser, not the tallest — the most RELIABLE, which in geysers is the miracle. Old Faithful has erupted every 44 to 125 minutes for as long as records exist: superheated water at 118°C forced through a pressure-tight rock plumbing system that reloads like clockwork, 30–55 m high for 1.5–5 minutes.
see: The eruption, obviously — but walk 10 min up Observation Point for the view above the crowd, or watch from the Inn's second-floor deck with a coffee
Old Faithful Inn (1904): the largest log building on Earth, its 23-m stone fireplace and gnarled-wood balconies built in a single winter — 15 minutes inside is mandatory
Predicted eruption times are posted at the visitor center and on signboards (±10 min)
tips: Arrive 20 min before the predicted time for a front-row bench seat

While waiting for the next eruption, walk the Upper Geyser Basin boardwalk behind it — the densest concentration of geysers on the planet; Castle, Grand and Riverside are all more spectacular than Old Faithful if they happen to go
Cell signal exists here (barely) — check @GeyserNPS for Grand Geyser predictions if it loads
d6s0.fact: Early park visitors used Old Faithful as a laundry: clothes stuffed in the vent came back washed and hot-pressed by the next eruption. Wool, reportedly, was shredded.

d6s1.what: Grand Prismatic is the largest hot spring in the US, third on Earth: 110 m across, deeper than a 10-storey building, and colored like a gas flame — sterile 87°C blue at the center, ringed in yellow, orange and rust by mats of heat-loving microbes, each ring a different species tuned to a different temperature.
see: The Overlook: from Fairy Falls trailhead, walk 1.2 km of flat trail then a short climb to the platform — THIS is the aerial photo you've seen; from ground level it's just steam
The microbial mats up close afterwards (optional): drive 2 min north to Midway Geyser Basin and walk the boardwalk past Excelsior Geyser's 55,000-litre-per-minute boiling outflow into the river
tips: Do the overlook FIRST from Fairy Falls TH — most crowds only do the boardwalk
Morning is best: cool air = more steam at ground level but the overlook still reads the colors; midday has least steam if you want maximum color
The trailhead lot fills by ~9:30am — another argument for your early start
d6s1.fact: The microbes painting those orange rings — thermophiles — were unknown to science until Yellowstone. One of them, Thermus aquaticus, gave us the enzyme behind the PCR test; every COVID test ever run traces back to this pool's cousins.

d6s2.what: The one boardwalk with all four geothermal feature types in 800 m: geysers (erupting water), hot springs (still water), fumaroles (steam vents screaming through cracks), and mudpots — acid pools that dissolve rock into gurgling clay soup.
see: The mudpots mid-loop — early summer they're thick and blooping like a cartoon cauldron
Clepsydra Geyser near the exit: it erupts almost constantly, your guaranteed geyser photo
Dead lodgepole 'bobby-sock trees' — killed by shifting hot ground, white ankles of absorbed silica
tips: 30–45 min, flat and easy — a perfect between-basins stop
Stay ON the boardwalk everywhere in this park: the crust beside it can be centimetres thick over boiling water; people and animals have died proving that
d6s2.fact: The paint pots' colors come from minerals: iron oxides tint the clay pink and grey. Early visitors genuinely used the mud as paint — hence the name.

d6s3.what: The hottest, oldest and most volatile thermal basin in the park — 115,000 years of activity, ground temperatures just below boiling, and water so acidic it dissolves boardwalk nails. Norris sits on the intersection of three fault lines; everything here changes year to year, sometimes overnight.
see: Porcelain Basin: a milky-blue mineral plain hissing with dozens of vents — the most alien landscape in Yellowstone
Steamboat Geyser: the tallest active geyser on Earth (90–120 m, three times Old Faithful). Eruptions are totally unpredictable — but even its constant minor spitting is worth the walk
Echinus Geyser: the largest acid geyser known, pH of vinegar
tips: Do Porcelain Basin loop (30 min) if tired, both loops (1.5 h) if not
This is your last basin — by now you can read them: blue = hottest, orange = cooler microbes, clear = acid
Afternoon here then exit via Madison to West Yellowstone — bison herds often line the Madison River in evening light
d6s3.fact: When Steamboat does fully erupt, it rains sand and rock chips on the parking lot and cars need washing. Owners of wet cars are considered lottery winners — some people chase this geyser for years.

d7s0.what: A geyser basin INSIDE the lake: West Thumb is a caldera-within-the-caldera, a smaller volcanic explosion 174,000 years ago that the lake flooded. Hot springs boil straight up through the cold lake floor along the shore — the collision of the two worlds is the whole show.
see: Fishing Cone: a geyser cone poking out of the lake — 1870s anglers stood on it, caught trout, and cooked them in the vent without moving ('hook and cook'); banned now, legendary forever
Abyss Pool: 16 m deep, the color of the deep end of the universe
Black Pool: no longer black — it cooked its dark microbes in a 1991 heat surge and turned impossible turquoise
tips: 45 min, flat boardwalk, right on your route south — the perfect goodbye to Yellowstone
Morning light on the lake + steam = your best photos here
Elk graze the basin edges most mornings
d7s0.fact: The lake bottom just offshore has its own geysers and hot vents — kayak tours float over them; the water is 5°C on top and boiling 3 m down.

d7s1.what: In 1942 Ansel Adams stood roughly here and made 'The Tetons and the Snake River' — one of the most reproduced landscape photographs ever taken; it's aboard both Voyager spacecraft as a message to whoever finds them. The Tetons are the youngest range in the Rockies (10 million years, still rising) with no foothills — they simply detonate 2,100 m out of the valley floor along a fault.
see: The river bend + full range panorama — pines have grown since 1942, so Adams' exact frame is gone, but the sweep is intact
Grand Teton (4,199 m) — the sharp one; Mount Moran to the right with the black basalt dike running up its face like a zipper
tips: 15-minute stop; morning is best — the range faces east and lights up front-on
Haze from distant fires is common in July; it makes sunsets better and midday flatter
d7s1.fact: 'Teton' is what lonely French trappers named these peaks — 'les Trois Tétons,' the three breasts. The Shoshone name, Teewinot, 'many pinnacles,' was better, but the French wrote the maps.

d7s2.what: Two rows of 1890s homesteads built by Mormon settlers who came north from Idaho, aligned along a central lane per Brigham Young's town-planning doctrine. The T.A. Moulton barn — the gambrel-roofed one with the full Teton wall behind it — is routinely called the most photographed barn in America.
see: T.A. Moulton barn (south row) — THE photo; his brother John's barn (north row, by the pink stucco house) is the other one
Pronghorn and bison herds graze the flats here most mornings
The old irrigation ditches and lilac bushes — 130-year-old landscaping still outliving its planters
tips: 30 min; the dirt road is fine for the truck
Photographers swarm at dawn; late morning you'll have it nearly alone (light's still good — the barns face east)
d7s2.fact: It took T.A. Moulton 30 years to finish his barn. It has now been famous for longer than it was ever used for hay.

d7s3.what: Jackson's town square is framed by four arches built entirely of elk antlers — about 2,000 antlers each, 14 tonnes of them, collected since 1953 by Boy Scouts from the National Elk Refuge just north of town, where up to 10,000 elk winter. Elk shed antlers annually; nobody is harmed except aesthetically.
see: The four antler arches (best photo: northeast corner at dusk when the string lights come on)
The Town Square 'Shootout' — a gloriously hammy Old West gunfight staged most summer evenings around 6pm (skip guilt-free if timing is off)
Million Dollar Cowboy Bar across the street: saddles for bar stools, live country, tourists and actual ranch hands in the same room
tips: Parking is tight; the free public garage a block north usually has space
Dinner here is your splurge-adjacent option; Victor, ID (if that's your bed) has the excellent Knotty Pine for half the price
Gas up tonight — tomorrow starts early
d7s3.fact: The scouts auction surplus antlers on the square each May; a single matched pair from a big bull can fetch over $1,000, and the money funds the refuge that feeds the elk that grow next year's arches.

d8s0.what: Jenny Lake fills a bowl that a glacier gouged directly at the foot of the steepest face of the Tetons — 130 m deep, absurdly clear, and the front door to the range's best short hike. The boat shuttle across (running since the 1950s) saves you 3.5 km of shoreline walking each way and is a highlight itself.
see: Hidden Falls: 60 m of whitewater 800 m from the west boat dock — an easy walk in cathedral forest
Inspiration Point: 1 km further, 120 m above the lake — the whole valley (Jackson Hole) laid out below, with the lake like poured glass
If legs are fresh: even 1 km into Cascade Canyon beyond the point is the best-value alpine scenery of the entire trip — moose live in the willows there
tips: First boats ~7am, every 10–15 min, ~$12/person round trip, card only — be on an early one, the trail is a conveyor belt by 10
This is grizzly country for real: talk as you walk (they avoid noisy humans); bear spray is rentable in Jackson if you want it
Water and sunscreen — the climb to Inspiration Point is short but honest
Be back at the truck by ~12:30 for the drive to SLC
d8s0.fact: The lake is named for Jenny Leigh, a Shoshone woman married to English trapper 'Beaver Dick' Leigh — she guided the 1872 Hayden survey that mapped this valley. She and all six children died of smallpox four years later; the loveliest lake in the Rockies is her monument.

d8s1.what: Recovery night in the Crossroads of the West. SLC sits between the Wasatch Front and the Great Salt Lake, 1,300 m up — founded in 1847 by Brigham Young and 148 pioneers as a refuge 1,000 km from anywhere, planned from day one on a grid whose streets are numbered by distance from the temple.
see: Nothing mandatory tonight — that's the point. Eat something that isn't road food
If restless: the State Capitol at dusk has the best free view over the valley
tips: Hotels near downtown/Temple Square put tomorrow's stroll on foot
Grocery stores are everywhere and huge — restock the cooler tonight or tomorrow morning
Utah quirk: beer over 5% is state-store only; restaurants pour full-strength with food
d8s1.fact: The street grid's addresses ('300 South 200 East') are coordinates — locals navigate the whole city by mental math from the temple's front door.
